name: Ashwatthama
description: Ashwatthama, a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, is the son of Drona and Kripi. He is considered one of the seven immortals (Chiranjivis) and plays a significant role in the epic Mahabharata. Born with a gem in his forehead, Ashwatthama possesses immense strength and martial prowess, traits inherited from his father, Drona, a renowned warrior and teacher. His fierce loyalty to his father and his unwavering commitment to his principles often lead him down a path of vengeance and destruction. Ashwatthama's actions during and after the Kurukshetra War are marked by cruelty and a disregard for dharma, ultimately resulting in a curse that condemns him to eternal suffering and wandering. His character embodies the complexities of dharma, karma, and the consequences of unchecked rage and ambition. He serves as a cautionary figure, highlighting the dangers of prioritizing personal loyalty over universal principles of righteousness.
subtitle: The Immortal Warrior

keyMyths.title: Ashwatthama's Birth
keyMyths.description: Drona longed for a son with the same martial prowess as himself. He prayed to Lord Shiva, who granted him Ashwatthama, born with a gem on his forehead that bestowed upon him immense strength and protection. This birth fulfilled Drona's desire for a son who would continue his legacy as a formidable warrior, setting the stage for Ashwatthama's future role in the Mahabharata and the events that would define his destiny.

keyMyths.title: The Death of Drona
keyMyths.description: During the Kurukshetra War, Krishna orchestrated a plan to break Drona's spirit by spreading the rumor of Ashwatthama's death. Bhima killed an elephant named Ashwatthama, and Yudhishthira, known for his truthfulness, reluctantly confirmed 'Ashwatthama is dead,' but whispered 'the elephant.' Drona, believing his son was dead, lost his will to fight and was subsequently killed. This deceit deeply affected Ashwatthama and fueled his desire for revenge.

keyMyths.title: The Brahmastra and the Curse
keyMyths.description: Consumed by rage after the war, Ashwatthama unleashed the Brahmastra, a powerful divine weapon, upon the Pandava camp, aiming to kill Parikshit, Arjuna's grandson, who was still in Uttara's womb. Krishna intervened and saved Parikshit but cursed Ashwatthama to wander the earth for eternity, afflicted with incurable sores and deprived of human contact. The gem on his forehead, his source of power, was also removed. This curse marked the culmination of Ashwatthama's transgressions and sealed his fate as an immortal outcast.

sources.author: Ganguli, Kisari Mohan
sources.description: A complete translation of the Mahabharata, providing detailed accounts of Ashwatthama's actions and motivations.

sources.author: Hiltebeitel, Alf
sources.work: Rethinking the Mahabharata: A Reader's Guide to the Education of the Dharma King
sources.description: Offers a scholarly analysis of the Mahabharata's complex themes, including the role of characters like Ashwatthama in exploring dharma and its contradictions.
sources.text: Offers a scholarly analysis of the Mahabharata's complex themes, including the role of characters like Ashwatthama in exploring dharma and its contradictions.

sources.author: Doniger, Wendy
sources.description: A compilation of Hindu myths, providing context and interpretations of Ashwatthama's story within the broader framework of Hindu mythology.
